trajectory_summary: The call is structurally compliant but energetically flat. Holland moves through verify items competently, clears a service-area check, and lands on the correct bill-collection pivot. What she never does is build any momentum: the bill pain ($500–$700, all-electric) is acknowledged and dropped, the 40-year tenure is noted and dropped, and the call ends with a soft open loop rather than a locked next step. The customer is no more engaged at 07:31 than she was at 00:06.
outcome_dispute: The v1 outcome tag is listed as 'unknown.' Based on the transcript, the call ends with a text-follow-up promise and no booked appointment — this is a tentative/conditional outcome, not a confirmed sit. The open loop (bill photo pending, no time anchor) makes this a fragile lead. If this is a paid lead, the appointment cannot be set until the bill is received, so the outcome is correctly 'pending bill.' If organic, Holland could have booked the appointment now and deferred bill collection — in which case the outcome is a missed booking opportunity. The manager should tag this as 'pending — bill follow-up required' and confirm lead type.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Bill anchor depth — capture without flip
primary_coaching_focus.quote: Well, I've you know, I have been checking it around, you know, you know, and none of that. My electric bill keeps going up.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Darla hands Holland the two highest-leverage elements of the call in the same 30 seconds: she's been shopping (competitive context) and her bill is the driver (emotional anchor). Holland acknowledges both in two sentences and moves to utility company. The schema's bill_anchoring section is explicit that capture alone is not sufficient — the bill must be flipped into emotional alignment against the utility. This pattern repeats at 03:06 when Darla discloses $500–$700 all-electric and Holland responds with 'Oh my goodness' and moves on. The flip was available both times and Holland never took it. This is the universal floor coaching gap the schema identifies, and it's the highest-leverage single skill Holland can develop on this call.

key_moments.quote: Well, I've you know, I have been checking it around, you know, you know, and none of that. My electric bill keeps going up.
key_moments.stewart_read: Darla volunteers her motivation unprompted — she's been shopping around and her bill is the driver. The schema marks interest_in_solar as the highest-weighted verify item with explicit depth_grading: 'Top setters spend 60+ seconds here.' Holland's response is two sentences of sympathy and an immediate pivot to utility company. The emotional anchor — the reason Darla is on this call at all — is acknowledged and abandoned. This is not a strategic deferral; there is no later moment in the transcript where Holland returns to Darla's motivation.

Fifteen years is the schema's hard_dq_if threshold for roof age. Holland captures the number and moves on without probing whether Darla has replacement plans. Two things are missed simultaneously: (1) the DQ check — is this roof actually over 15 years, and if so, is replacement planned? Darla said 'about fifteen,' which could mean 14 or 16; Holland never clarifies. (2) The roofing cross-sell signal — if the roof needs replacement, Ion does roofing in-house, and this is a bridge opportunity. The schema's cross_sell_signals.roof_replacement_planned section explicitly flags this scenario.

key_moments.quote: Sometimes it's 700. Sometimes it's 500. Let's see. I have all electric.
key_moments.stewart_read: Darla volunteers a $500–$700 range and the all-electric detail in the same breath — this is the highest-value data point in the call. Holland's response is 'Oh my goodness' followed by 'Yeah. Okay. Alrighty.' The schema's bill_anchoring section is explicit: capture alone is not sufficient — the bill must be flipped into emotional alignment against the utility. Holland captures and drops. The flip was available: Darla's own words ('my electric bill keeps going up,' said at 02:41) plus the $500–$700 range plus all-electric is a complete setup for the inversion move. Holland never takes it.

key_moments.quote: Sorry. Is there I'm just asking. So it looks like you're just a tiny, tiny, tiny bit out of our bounds. So I'm just reaching out to see if it would be okay if we work with you.
key_moments.stewart_read: Holland discloses a service-area boundary issue to Darla without any prepared framing. 'Tiny, tiny, tiny bit out of our bounds' is improvised and anxiety-signaling — the triple repetition communicates uncertainty, not confidence. Darla has no context for what 'out of our bounds' means, why it was checked, or what the resolution means for her. The issue resolves ('everything looks good') but Holland never explains what was checked or why Darla should feel confident proceeding. This is a trust moment that was handled procedurally but not relationally.
